titleStmt.title: Superconductivity with a Violation of Pauli Limit and Evidences for Multigap in -Carbide type Ti 4 Ir 2 O

analytic.title: Superconductivity with a Violation of Pauli Limit and Evidences for Multigap in -Carbide type Ti 4 Ir 2 O

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Ti 4 Ir 2 O superconductivity Pauli limit -carbide heat capacity multigap correlation

div.head: Introduction
div.p: For their superior abilities to carry large currents, superconductors have been widely used to generate high magnetic fields at different settings, such as medical magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), maglevs, particle accelerators, etc. [1,2]. However, applications to these uses have been limited to only a few superconducting compounds, primarily NbTi or Nb 3 Sn [3]. Apart from the superconducting transition temperature ( ), the critical current density, and the ductibility, one major limitation comes from the upper critical field ( 2 ) in a type-II superconductor.
At low temperatures, the Zeeman energy splitting (Δ ) is the key factor limiting 2 . When the external magnetic field is large enough (≥ 0

), driving Δ comparable with twice of the superconducting energy gap, the Cooper pairs are broken. In a Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) weak coupling scenario, the Pauli paramagnetic limit (or, Pauli limit for short) is expressed by 0 = 1.86 × (T/K).

2 of most type-II superconductors falls below , regardless of the value of [4]. While a violation of the Pauli limit is often regarded as a side evidence for unconventional superconductivity, as in heavy fermions [5], iron-based superconductors [6], non-centrosymmetric superconductors [7,8], and more recently, in 2 Cr 3 As 3 ( = Na, K, Rb, Cs) [9,10,11] or magic-angle twisted trilayer graphene [12].

In addition to unconventional superconductivity, large spin-orbit scattering [13], significant electron correlations, strong electron-phonon coupling [14], or a highly anisotropic structure [15] all tend to increase 2 , pushing it beyond . Nevertheless, superconductors with a violation of Pauli limit are still very rare.

In this study, we focus on a suboxide of the so-called " -carbide" family, also known as the E9 3 -type phases. The -carbide type phase adopts a cubic crystal structure of the space group 3 (No. 227), which is derived from the Ti 2 Ni type after small atoms, typically C, N, or O, take the interstitial sites. W 3 Fe 3 C, the first member in the family, was discovered in the 1950's [16,17]. Later on, a large number of carbides, nitrides, and oxides of the same structural family have been reported. Depending on different crystallographic sites the atoms take, there are four stoichiometries of -carbide type phases, namely (T and T' stand for transition metals, X = C, N, O): (a) T 4 T' 2 X, as in Mo . Although they have been discovered for more than half a century, reports on these phases mainly focus on their hardness [20], the hydrogen-sorption [21], or the catalytic abilities [22], few of them has been examined for their electronic properties.

Ku et al. reported several -carbide type superconductors in 1984 [23], including Ti [24] and Nb 4 Rh 2 C 1- [25] was systematically studied. Nb 4 Rh 2 C 1-was found to host a large 0 2 (0) of 28.5 T, violating the Pauli paramagnetic limit. This is not trivial since -carbide type phases are not highly anisotropic in their crystal structures. Thus, strong electron correlations, or even unconventional superconductivity can be expected in the superconductors of -carbide type. However, evidences for these speculations are still absent.

Here we report the synthesis and a systematical study of superconductivity in Ti 4 Ir 2 O. Bulk superconductivity of = 5.12 K is revealed by magnetic susceptibility and heat capacity measurements, confirming a previous report by Matthias et al. in 1963[26]. In that paper, superconductivity in "Ti 0.573 Ir 0.287 O 0.14 " was reported without further details. Notice that such an early study needs reexamination. For example, superconductivity of = 11.8 K in "Zr 0.61 Rh 0.285 O 0.105 " reported in the same paper [26] was later ascribed to CuAl 2 -type Zr 2 Rh [27]. In addition, superconducting parameters of Ti 4 Ir 2 O are determined in our study, among which the 0 2 (0) is 16.45 T, far above the Pauli limit (9.52 T). Moreover, the deviation of 0 2 ( ) from the Werthamer-Helfand-Hohenberg (WHH) theory, and the heat capacity in the superconducting state suggest a multigap nature. Large electron correlations in Ti 4 Ir 2 O are evidenced by both experimental results and first-principles calculations.

div.head: Methods
div.p: Polycrystalline samples of Ti 4 Ir 2 O were prepared by solid state reactions. Stoichiometric amount of titanium (powder, 99.99%), iridium (powder, 99.99%), and TiO 2 (powder, 99.95%) was mixed thoroughly, and then cold pressed into pellets. The pellets were placed in corundum crucibles, which were subsequently sealed into niobium tubes under purified argon. The niobium tubes were heated to 1800 K, held at the temperature for 20 hours, and then cooled down to room temperature by switching off the furnace. To avoid the oxidation of niobium tubes at high temperatures, heating treatments were carried out under an argon atmosphere. Grey and dense solid was obtained after opening the niobium tubes. No reaction between the samples and the niobium tubes was observed, and the weight losses after the reactions were negligible. We also performed reactions at lower temperatures or using arc-melting methods. However, these alternative methods did not produce singlephase samples. (see Supporting Information) Powder x-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were carried out on a PAN-analytical x-ray diffractometer equipped with Cu-K radiation at room temperature. The Rietveld refinement of the XRD results were performed with the GSAS package [28]. Temperature dependent electrical resistivity and heat capacity of the sample was measured on a Quantum Design physical property measurement system (PPMS). DC magnetization was measured on a Quantum Design magnetic property measurement system (MPMS). Details for the physical property measurements can be found elsewhere [29]. All the data from the magnetization measurements were corrected based on the demagnetization factor, which was determined by the dimensions of the rectangular sample [30].

First-principles calculations were carried out in the frame of the density functional theory (DFT), using the QUANTUM ESPRESSO (QE) package [31,32,33]. Generalized gradient approximation (GGA) exchange-correlation functionals of PBEsol [34] were selected, together with the projector augmented wave pseudopotentials from the PSLIBRARY package [35]. To take spin-orbit coupling (SOC) into account, we performed both the scalar-relativistic and fully-relativistic calculations. Energy cut-offs were 100 Ry and 1000 Ry for the wavefunctions and the charge densities, respectively. A Monkhorst-Pack grid of 8 3 -points was used for the self-consistent calculation, and a denser grid of 12 3 -points was used to generate the density of states (DOS). Before the self-consistent calculation, the cell parameters, as well as the atomic positions, were fully relaxed starting from the experimental values, using the Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS) algorithm. The grid of -mesh was checked for convergence. The Barder analysis was performed with the help of the CRITIC2 package [36].Fermi surfaces were constructed on a superfine grid of 101 3 -points, with the help of maximally localized Wannier functions (MLWFs) generated by the WANNIER90 code [37,38]. 1). We notice that the inclusion of oxygen is key to stablize the Ti 4 Ir 2 O phase, since no Ti 2 Ni-type "Ti 2 Ir" phase can be acquired (nor has it been reported in the Ti-Ir phase diagram [39]).

div.head: Results
div.p: The crucial roles of interstitial C, N, or O have been frequently observed in other -carbide type phases, such as
div.formula: W 3 Fe 3 C[17], Zr 4 Pd 2 N[23], or Zr 4 Rh 2 O[24].
div.p: Rietveld refinement was performed and the result is shown in Figure 1, giving cell parameters = = = 11.6194(1) Å. The value is close to the reference one 11.620 Å [26].The occupancy on each site is refined to be 1.0 (except for that of oxygen, which is fixed to 1. We will come back to this issue in Section 4). Detailed refined crystallographic parameters are summarized in Table 1. The fully relaxed cell parameters and atomic positions are also listed for comparison. Notice that the discrepancies between the DFT values and the experimental values are within 0.6%, and the inclusion of SOC has negligible effects on the crystallographic parameters.

Superconductivity is evidenced in both resistivity and magnetization measurements, as shown in Figure 2. At the normal state, Ti 4 Ir 2 O displays a metallic behavior, with the resistivity ( ) decreases upon cooling. The residual resistivity ratio ( = (300 K)/ (6 K) = 1.39) is quite small, which is typical for a transition metal suboxide (for example, see [24,40]). The normal state ( ) shows a convex feature ("saturation") above 50 K. This can be explained with a parallel-resistor model, in which a strong electronphonon coupling takes place (see, for instance, the A15 superconductors [41,42] and SrPt 3 P [43]). Alternatively, it can be interpreted by an inter-band scattering, known as the Bloch-Grüneisen-Mott (BGM) mechanism [29,44,45]. The inset of Figure 2 shows ( ) below 70 K, in which we find that the data below 35 K following a Fermi liquid behavior:
giving 0 = 0.263 mΩ cm, = 10.25 nΩ cm K -2 . At lower temperatures, ( ) starts to drop from 5.22 K ( ), reaching zero at 5.13 K (
), indicating the occurrence of superconductivity. Such a sharp transition implies a superior homogeneity of our sample.

In Figure 2(b), we show the DC magnetic susceptibility ( ) of Ti 4 Ir 2 O from 1.8 K to 8.0 K. The large diamagnetic signal in the zero-field cooling (ZFC) process confirms the bulk nature of the superconductivity. The field cooling (FC) gives a much smaller diamagnetism, suggesting a substantial pinning effect in the sample.
determined from the magnetization measurement is 5.12 K, which is in very good agreement with the value from ( ).

Figure 3(a) shows the superconducting transition under various magnetic fields. Clearly, the transition is suppressed to lower temperatures when applying external fields. However, the suppression of is very slow. For instance, under a magnetic field of 9 T, is still above 2.5 K. Temperature dependence of the heat capacity ( ) shows a distinct anomaly at low temperatures (Figure 3(d)), which again validates the bulk superconductivity. can be determined from the ∕ -curves based on an entropy balance method. At zero magnetic field, determined from is 5.12 K, which is in excellent agreement with the values from ( ) and ( ).

Results from ( ) and ∕ -under various magnetic fields allow us to determine 2 of Ti 4 Ir 2 O. In Figure 3(f), the points from the ( ) measurements are determined from the midpoints of the superconducting transitions, while points from ∕ -curves are determined using the method described above, error bars correspond to the transition width. The data from ( ) and ∕ -agree well with each other, while the transition widths are larger in the measurements. The Pauli paramagnetic limit for a superconductor with = 5.12 K is ∼ 9.52 T. However, as shown in Figure 3(f), superconductivity still survives when the magnetic field is 12 T. This conclusively indicates a violation of Pauli limit in Ti 4 Ir 2 O.
The fit to 2 is carried out using the WHH relation [46]:
div.formula: in which = ∕ is the normalized temperature, ℎ = -(4∕ 2 ) 2 ( )∕[ ( 2 ∕ )| = ]
div.p: is the dimensionless magnetic field, and ( ) is the digamma function. =

( 2 ℎ 2 -2 SO ∕4) 1∕2 , where is the Maki parameter, and SO is the parameter measuring the spin-orbit scattering. WHH fit of 2 gives: = 1.90, SO = 14.05. Such a large SO means that there is little Pauli limiting effect in Ti 4 Ir 2 O, and the spin-orbit scattering dominates. However, as shown in Figure 3(f), it fails to reproduce the 2 data under magnetic fields beyond 7 T, yielding an underestimated 0 2 (0). In fact, SO ≫ 1 also implies that WHH theory, which assumes a small SO , is not suitable in our case. The failure of WHH fit comes from the linearity feature of the temperature dependence of 2 . Such a deviation can be explained by multigap superconductivity, as observed in MgB 2 [47], 2 -NbSe 2 [48], iron-based superconductors [6], and YRe 2 SiC [49].Indeed, the 2 data is excellently reproduced by a two-band model developed by Gurevich [50], as indicated by the purple line in Figure 3(f). This is not surprising because the two-band model is based on many fitting parameters. To gain insights into the nature of the intra-band and inter-band scattering, detailed studies such as nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) or muon spectroscopy are needed.

Alternatively, 2 can also be fitted with the phenomenological Ginzburg-Landau (G-L) relation: 0 2 ( ) 2 . The G-L fit is also shown in Figure 3(f), giving 0 2 (0) = 16.45 T, which is much larger than the Pauli paramagnetic limit.

The isothermal magnetization ( -) curves, shown in Figure 3(b), give us knowledge about the lower critical field ( 1 ). The magnetic field where the curve starts to depart from the initial Meissner states is determined as 1 , which is plotted in Figure 3(c). A G-L fit is carried out with the formula:

giving 0 1 (0) = 13.18 mT. A full magnetization loop at 2.0 K is shown as the inset of Figure 3 Based on the value of 1 (0) and 2 (0), a bunch of superconducting parameters, such as the G-L coherence length (
), the penetration depth ( ), the G-L parameter (

), and the thermodynamic field ( (0)) can be estimated. These parameters are summarized in Table 2. Details for calculating the parameters can be found in our previous study [29]. Notice the value of is close to the isostructural superconductor Nb 4 Rh 2 C 1- [25], and is far beyond 1∕ √ 2, suggesting an extreme type-II character. Inset of Figure 3(e) shows the heat capacity under zero field, and under a field of 13.5 T, which totally suppress the superconducting transition above 1.8 K. The normal state data can be fitted with Debye model: ( ) = + 3 + 5 , in which the three terms are the electronic contribution to the heat capacity ( ), the harmonic phonon contribution, and the anharmonic phonon contribution, respectively. The fitting parameters are: = 33.74 mJ mol -1 K -2 , = 0.265 mJ mol -1 K -4 , and = 2.53 J mol -1 K -6 . Debye temperature (Θ ) can be obtained by:

in which is the number of atoms per formula, and is the ideal gas constant. From equation(3) we get Θ = 372 K. According to the McMillan relation [51], the electronphonon coupling strength ( ) can be expressed by: )] = 8.83 eV -1 per formula unit (f. u.). gives us more insights about the nature of the superconductivity. To exclude the contribution of any possible impurities, is obtained by subtracting | 0 =0 + on | 0 =13.5 T . The result is shown in Figure 3(e). At the superconducting state, quickly approaches zero, indicating a nodeless feature of the superconducting gap. Therefore, fit to the is carried out based on isotropic -wave models. However, a one-gap -wave model fails to describe at the superconducting state. Entropy balance at the superconducting transition is not fulfilled either. On the other hand, a two-gap -wave model perfectly describes
1.29 ( ) (eV -1 per f. u.) 8.83 experimental value deduced from . ′ ( ) (eV -1 per f. u.) 7.26 theoretical value from DFT calculation with SOC.

the behavior, while maintaining the entropy balance. The two superconducting gaps are determined to be Δ 1 = 1.37 meV, and Δ 2 = 0.57 meV. Notice that the larger one is above the BCS weak coupling value (Δ 1 ∕ = 3.10 > 1.76). This again indicates an enhanced electron-phonon coupling in Ti 4 Ir 2 O, which is further evidenced by the large jump at (Δ ∕ = 1.77). Results from the first principles calculation are shown in Figure 4, with electronic band structures in Figure 4(a) and DOS in Figure 4(b) and (c). Both the scalar relativistic (w/o SOC) and the fully relativistic (with SOC) results are shown. In both cases, there are five bands crossing the Fermi level, consistent with the metallic nature of Ti 4 Ir 2 O. The existence of multiple bands crossing also makes multigap superconductivity plausible. Notice that the energy dispersion near is dramatically changed when SOC is turned on. For example, band splitting occurs along the Γ-X, Γ-K, and L-W lines. This makes the Fermi surface topology very different when considering SOC (see Figure S5). Most absorbingly, nesting between the electron-like and hole-like pockets becomes prominent when SOC is turned on.

The Barder analysis was based on the converged charge densities, resulting in a valence configuration of Ti the formation of Ti-O bonds. DOS at is slightly lowered with SOC, giving a theoretical ′ ( ) = 7.26 eV -1 per f. u.. This value is comparable with the one calculated from . The slightly enhancement of experimental ( ) is possibly due to the electron-electron correlations. Notice that locates near a pronounced peak of DOS, which arises from the relatively flat energy dispersion of the bands near . After the projection to each atomic orbital, we find that DOS at originates mainly from the Ti-3 orbitals, with minor contributions from the Ir-5 components.

div.head: Discussion and conclusion
div.p: First we would like to discuss the stoichiometry of Ti 4 Ir 2 O, because -carbide type phases frequently host vacancies on the interstitial sites [24,25]. According to the preparation procedure and the XRD results, however, the oxygen vacancies should be very close to zero. On the other hand, there should be a tiny amount of oxygen vacancies, since samples prepared at 1250 K were found to host smaller lattices and an enhanced of 5.7 K (see Supporting Information). In -carbide type suboxides, the lattice shrinks when more oxygen is incorporated, as observed in Zr 4 Rh 2 O 0.6 [24]. This means that the oxygen contents in the 5.7 K samples are larger. The change of should be attributed to the slight non-stoichiometry of Ti 4 Ir 2 O, given that DOS has a steep slope at . In other words, a small amount of electron doping can dramatically change the value of ( ). For example, ( ) would be reduced to 6.80 eV -1 per f. u. if 10% oxygen vacancies were introduced (assuming a rigid band model). The evolution of upon vacancies requires detailed doping studies in the future.

Next we focus on the origin of the large 2 . One possible cause of the enhancement of 2 is the spin-triplet component in the order parameter, which can be induced by non-centrosymmetric crystal structures [7,8]. However, this should not be the case since Ti 4 Ir 2 O hosts a centrosymmetric crystal structure. Another possibility of enhancement due to anisotropy, as observed in many low dimensional superconductors, such as Li 0.9 Mo 6 O 17 [52], LaO 0.5 F 0.5 BiS 2 [15], La 2 IRu 2 [53], and K 2 Mo 3 As 3 [54], is also ruled out. Because both the crystal structure and the Fermi surfaces (see Figure S5) are clearly three-dimensional.

The reduction of -factor can cause a violation of the Pauli limit. To elucidate this, a fit to the normal state magnetization data is carried out based on ( ) = 0 + ∕( -), giving a temperature independent term of 0 = 1.73 × 10 -3 emu mol -1 . After subtracting the core diamagnetic contributions from Ti 4+ , Ir 4+ , and O 2-, we obtain an estimation of the Pauli paramagnetic susceptibility ∼ 1.84 × 10 -3 emu mol -1 . Therefore, the Wilson ratio = 2 2 ∕(3 2 ) ∼ 3.9. Such a large indicates the existence of strong electron correlation and/or a low-lying magnetic order [55,56]. The strong electron correlation is further evidenced by the Kadowaki-Woods ratio ∕ 2 = 9.0 × 10 -6 Ω cm/(mJ mol -1 K -1 ) 2 , which is comparable with those in heavy fermion systems (for instance, 1.0 × 10 -5 Ω cm/(mJ mol -1 K -1 ) 2 for UPt 3 [5] and CeCu 2 Si 2 [57]). It is also probably the cause for the enhancement of compared with the theoretical value. Finally, since the Pauli limit is based on the BCS weak coupling gap value of 1.76 , a strong coupling can also enhance it. In our case, Δ 1 ∕ = 3.10 is indeed beyond the BCS weak coupling limit. However, superconductors with similar coupling strength, such as the A15 compounds[41, Ti 4 Ir 2 O 42] or SrPt 3 P [43] do not necessarily host a large 2 . We argue that the combination of strong-coupled superconductivity, substantial spin-orbit scattering, and the existence of strong electron correlation altogether result in the violation of Pauli limit.

To summarize, we report a detailed investigation of superconductivity in Ti 4 Ir 2 O. Being a strong-coupled superconductor with = 5.1 ∼ 5.7 K, Ti 4 Ir 2 O exhibits many intriguing properties. It has a large 0 2 (0) of 16.45 T, which is beyond the Pauli paramagnetic limit. A multigap superconductivity is evidenced by the temperature dependence of 2 , the heat capacity measurements, and the DFT calculations. Moreover, a strong electron correlation in Ti 4 Ir 2 O, which is comparable with typical heavy fermion superconductors, is revealed by the large values of the Wilson ratio and the Kadowaki-Woods ratio, as well as the relatively large Sommerfeld coefficient ( ). These results make Ti 4 Ir 2 O a rare example to study the interplay between strong electron correlation and multigap superconductivity. We notice that these results also strongly suggest unconventional superconductivity. Questions like whether there are spin fluctuations, or a direct observation of the multigap feature through spectroscopy methods are still open, calling for further studies. Given the fact that there are numerous -carbide type compounds, most of which are not systematically investigated for their transport properties, detailed studies in this structural family may flourish in the near future.

figure.figDesc: Co 2 C, Zr 4 Re 2 O[17];(b) T 3 T' 3 X, as in W 3 Fe 3 C, Nb 3 Cr 3 N[16];(c) T 6 T' 6 X, as in Mo 6 Ni 6 C[18]; (d) T 8 T' 4 X, as in Nb 8 Zn 4 C[19]
Co 2 O ( = 3.1 K), Zr 4 Os 2 O ( = 3.0 K), Nb 4 Rh 2 C ( = 8.9 K), etc.. Recently, superconductivity in Zr 4 Rh 2 O 0.6

figure.head: Figure 1
figure.figDesc: Figure 1 displays the powder XRD pattern of Ti 4 Ir 2 O collected at room temperature. We find that Ti 4 Ir 2 O crystallizes in an -carbide type structure, which is of the space group 3 (No. 227). In the structure, Ti and Ir form a Ti 2 Ni-type framework, where O takes the 16 (distorted octahedral) interstitial sites (see the inset of Figure1). We notice that the inclusion of oxygen is key to stablize the Ti 4 Ir 2 O phase, since no Ti 2 Ni-type "Ti 2 Ir" phase can be acquired

figure.head: Figure 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 1: Room-temperature XRD pattern of Ti 4 Ir 2 O and its Rietveld refinements. obs. , calc. , and dif f. stand for the observed intensity, the calculated intensity, and the difference between, respectively. The vertical bars indicate the Bragg positions for -carbide type Ti 4 Ir 2 O. The conventional unit cell is shown as the inset.

figure.head: b
figure.figDesc: Ti1 = Ti1 = 0, Ti2 = Ti2 = 0.125, Ir = Ir = Ir , O = O = O .c is defined as one-third of the trace of the orthogonalized tensor.

figure.head: Figure 2 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 2: (a) Temperature dependence of resistivity of Ti 4 Ir 2 O under zero magnetic field. Normal state data below 35 K can be described by equation(1), as shown in the inset. (b) Temperature dependence of DC magnetic susceptibility of Ti 4 Ir 2 O at low temperatures. Inset shows the temperature dependence of the normal state magnetization from 8 K to 300 K.

figure.head: Figure 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 3: (a) Superconducting transition on ( ) under magnetic fields from 0 T to 9 T, the field increment between each curve is 1 T. (b) Isothermal magnetization under different temperatures. (c) Lower critical fields ( 1 ) under different temperatures, and the fit according to the G-L relation. Inset shows the isothermal magnetization loop at 2.0 K. (d) Low temperature heat capacity under magnetic fields from 0 T to 13.5 T. (e) Electronic contribution of heat capacity at zero magnetic field. Solid curves show fits based on a one-gap -wave model (blue), and a two-gap -wave model (red). Contributions from the two gaps are displayed as dotted curves. (f) Upper critical fields ( 2 ) under different temperatures, determined from ( ) and ( ). The fitting curves are based on the ( ) data, according to the G-L (red), WHH(dotted), and two-band (purple) model, respectively. The value of 0 2 (0) is from the G-L fit.
(c), indicating type-II superconductivity in Ti 4 Ir 2 O.

04 + * ln(Θ ∕1.45 ) (1 -0.62 * ) ln(Θ ∕1.45 ) -1.04 . (Setting the Coulomb screening parameter * to 0.13, a typical value for intermetallics, we get = 0.61, suggesting moderate coupling in Ti 4 Ir 2 O. The density of states (DOS) at the Fermi level ( ) is calculated with ( ) = 3 ∕[ 2 2 (1 +

figure.head: 2 O 1 . 40 -
figure.figDesc: . This means charge transferring from Ti to Ir and O. The charge density and electron localization function (ELF) maps are shown in Figure S4, from which ionic Ti-O bonds and metallic Ti-Ir bonds can be identified. From the projected DOS, shown in Figure 4(b) and (c), we notice the states from O are absent near . While they hybridize with states from Ti at ∼ -5.6 to -2 eV, indicating

figure.head: Ti 4 Figure 4 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 4: (a) Electronic band structures of Ti 4 Ir 2 O from DFT calculation. Dotted lines show the results without SOC, while the SOC results are shown as solid lines. Solid lines in colors other than red indicate the bands crossing the Fermi level. (b) Projected DOS without SOC. (c) Projected DOS with SOC.

figure.head: Figure S1 :
figure.label: S1
figure.figDesc: Figure S1: Representative XRD patterns of Ti4Ir2O samples prepared with different methods. From bottom to top: SSR (Solid state reaction) at 1800 K, SSR at 1250 K, SSR at 1200 K, and arc-melted samples. SSR at 1800 K produced single-phase sample, while the other three methods produced multi-phase samples. The lattice parameter a is slightly different too. Tetr. TiIr stands for tetragonal TiIr (space group P4/mmm). "↓" indicates an unidentified phase.

figure.head: Figure S2 :
figure.label: S2
figure.figDesc: Figure S2: Electrical and magnetic properties of prepared by SSR at 1200 K. Notice that although Tc of this sample is somewhat higher than the one shown in the main text, the transition width is larger, and the upper critical field is lower (but still above the Pauli limit), indicating a less good sample quality. (a) DC magnetic susceptibility under 10 Oe from 1.8 K to 8 K. (b) Isothermal magnetization curves at different temperatures. (c) Superconducting transition under different magnetic fields. (d) Upper critical fields at different temperatures, and its fit by G-L relation.

figure.head: Figure S3 :
figure.label: S3
figure.figDesc: Figure S3: DC magnetic susceptibility of Ti4Ir2O prepared by arc-melting method.

figure.head: Figure S4 :
figure.label: S4
figure.figDesc: Figure S4: Charge (left) and electron localization function (ELF) (right) of Ti4Ir2O calculated with SOC. The charge density is bound between 0 and 0.2 e/a0 3 (a0 is the Bohr radius), while ELF is bound between 0 and 1.

figure.head: Figure S5 :
figure.label: S5
figure.figDesc: Figure S5: Fermi surfaces of Ti4Ir2O (a) without and (b) with SOC.

figure.head: Table 1
figure.figDesc: Crystallographic parameters of Ti 4 Ir 2 O from Rietveld Refinement a of room temperature XRD. Parameters from first-principles calculations were also listed for comparison.

figure.head: Table 2
figure.figDesc: Normal state and superconducting parameters of Ti 4 Ir 2 O.

row.cell: from ( ). K to 5.7 K in samples pre-ranges from 5.1

div.head: Conflict of interest
div.p: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

div.head: Acknowledgments
div.p: The authors are grateful for the financial supports from the National Key Research and Development of China (Grant No. 2018YFA0704200, 2017YFA0302904, and 2018 YFA0305602), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12074414, 12074002, and 11774402). [58]. The superconducting parameters determined there agreed fairly well with ours. However, the existence of strong correlation, as well as the multigap superconductivity, was overlooked in that study.

div.head: Superconductivity with a Violation of Pauli Limit and Evidences for
Multigap in η-Carbide type Ti4Ir2O
div.p: Bin-Bin Ruan a, b, * , Meng-Hu Zhou a, b , Qing-Song Yang b, c , Ya-Dong Gu b, c , Ming-Wei Ma b, c , Gen-Fu Chen b, c , Zhi-An Ren b, c, #

analytic.title: To use or not to use cool superconductors

Superconductivity in the A15 structure
